comments:
Summary:
This paper studies non-uniform worst-case regret guarantees for multi-armed bandits: how small can the worst-case regret be for some ‘‘favored’’ arms and what is the cost for the remaining arms. The main result is a characterization (up to constants and log factors) of the Pareto frontier of achievable worst-case regret vectors in the stochastic K-armed bandit setting. A lower bound shows that favoring an arm incurs a large penalty for others (essentially an Ω(nK/B) trade-off), and an ‘‘unbalanced MOSS’’ algorithm is proposed that achieves the frontier up to constant factors. Extensions/variants for UCB-style indices (giving better problem-dependent performance) and a modification of Exp3 for the adversarial setting are also provided. Short experiments validate the qualitative behavior.

Strengths:
- Novel and clean question: focusing on per-arm worst-case regret and characterizing the Pareto frontier is an interesting and timely theoretical contribution.
- Strong theoretical results: lower bound and matching (up to constants/log factors) upper bounds in the stochastic setting. The work clearly contrasts the bandit case with the experts setting, revealing a striking negative result about favouritism in the bandit feedback model.
- The proposed unbalanced MOSS algorithm is a natural and well-motivated generalization of MOSS; the analysis uses standard but carefully adapted concentration and KL techniques.
- The paper situates itself well with prior work (MOSS, UCB, OC-UCB, experts literature) and discusses algorithmic trade-offs (minimax vs. problem-dependent regret).

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- The lower bound relies on a Gaussian noise assumption (author acknowledges this). While the paper suggests the proof can be generalized to Bernoulli, the restriction to Gaussian for the formal statement weakens universality; a clearer, formal treatment for bounded or Bernoulli noise would strengthen the claim.
- Constant factors in the statements are large (c2 = 252) and the author admits these are loose. While asymptotic characterisation is the main point, tighter constants or empirical evidence about practical effectiveness would help.
- The adversarial results are weaker: the paper indicates the matching upper/lower bounds in the adversarial case are unresolved. That leaves an incomplete picture for the adversarial model.
- Experiments are limited and synthetic; they confirm qualitative behavior but do not assess sensitivity to horizon, K, or realistic reward distributions. More exhaustive experiments would make the empirical case more convincing.
- Some technical assumptions are worth clearer exposition: e.g., the assumption sup_i,j µi - µj ≤ 1 (gaps bounded) and its implications; dependence on independence of noise and measurability conditions could be highlighted for completeness.

Suggestions:
- If possible, present a formal lower bound for bounded/Bernoulli rewards (or state precisely which parts extend and which do not) to make the lower bound more broadly applicable.
- Include additional experiments exploring different K, n, and reward families to show practical regimes where unbalanced MOSS is useful vs harmful.
- Discuss more explicitly the implications for practitioners: when (and how) one should choose the target B vector in practice; link to prior-weighting intuitions in Thompson sampling and whether similar phenomena occur.
- If space permits, add discussion on whether constants/log factors might be improved (beyond saying they can) and any obstacles to doing so.

Conclusion:
This paper makes a significant theoretical contribution to the understanding of per-arm worst-case regret trade-offs in bandits. The results are novel, the analysis is substantial, and the conclusions are surprising and relevant to the community. With some clarifications on assumptions and possibly an extension of the lower bound beyond Gaussian noise (or clearer exposition of that extension), the work would be stronger. I recommend acceptance (or dissemination) as an interesting and high-quality theoretical paper.
